coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon sphere-supported sand-rose like N-GQDs/NiCo2S4 structures with synergetic effect for development of hydrogen storage capacity
coredata.dc:creator: Masjedi-Arani, Maryam
Ghiyasiyan-Arani, Maryam
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: Porous carbon spheres (CSs) decorated with sand-rose shaped NiCo2S4/Co9S8/CoS and N- doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were prepared via one-step free-surfactant hydrothermal route. Ni-Co sulfide based materials (NCS) were created in the presence of Tioglycolic acid (TGA) as both sulfur source and capping agent. Altering of synthesis parameters involving metal salt type, TGA and NaOH amount help to control the sulfide-based materials features. The obtained 3D hierarchical N-GQD/NCS/CS core-double shell spheres were considered in terms of morphology, dimension and porosity. Different formed Ni-Co sulfide structures were evaluated for hydrogen storage capacity. The achieved 3D microspheres could be utilized as a favorable candidate in electrochemical hydrogen storage, incorporating the prominent electro-conductivity of N-GQDs and CSs carbonic materials and high capacity of sand-rose shaped NCS nanostructures. Carbon microspheres formed through rapid sonochemical approach and various linear amines were applied as conductive support. It was discovered that the presenting of N-GQDs prominently improves the discharge efficiency of the core-double shell spheres from 930 to 1244 mAh g−1 because of trap positions formed by both Nitrogen dopants and edge conditions which can adsorb charge transporters to rise the storage proficiency.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon sphere-supported sand-rose like N-GQDs/NiCo2S4 structures with synergetic effect for development of hydrogen storage capacity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan P. O. Box. 87317-51167, Iran

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have same contribution on this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Porous carbon spheres (CSs) decorated with sand-rose shaped NiCo2S4/Co9S8/CoS and N- doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were prepared via one-step free-surfactant hydrothermal route. Ni-Co sulfide based materials (NCS) were created in the presence of Tioglycolic acid (TGA) as both sulfur source and capping agent. Altering of synthesis parameters involving metal salt type, TGA and NaOH amount help to control the sulfide-based materials features. The obtained 3D hierarchical N-GQD/NCS/CS core-double shell spheres were considered in terms of morphology, dimension and porosity. Different formed Ni-Co sulfide structures were evaluated for hydrogen storage capacity. The achieved 3D microspheres could be utilized as a favorable candidate in electrochemical hydrogen storage, incorporating the prominent electro-conductivity of N-GQDs and CSs carbonic materials and high capacity of sand-rose shaped NCS nanostructures. Carbon microspheres formed through rapid sonochemical approach and various linear amines were applied as conductive support. It was discovered that the presenting of N-GQDs prominently improves the discharge efficiency of the core-double shell spheres from 930 to 1244 mAh g−1 because of trap positions formed by both Nitrogen dopants and edge conditions which can adsorb charge transporters to rise the storage proficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Significant attempts have been assigned to developing renewable energy sources due to problems created from fossil fuel [1]. Hydrogen energy is one of the best candidates as a clean energy source because of its effective energy density and environmental friendliness [2,3]. One of the crucial problems of hydrogen energy is to find a method to effectively, safely, reversibly and economically store the H2 at ambient temperature and pressure. Electrochemical hydrogen storage has been regarded via water electrolysis that the hydrogen evolution reaction occurs in cathode (2H+ + 2e− → H2) and the oxygen evolution reaction takes place in anode (O2 + 4H+ + 4e− → H2O) [4]. In these processes, nano electrocatalysts have the favorable role to minimize the overpotential and achieve fast kinetics [5,6]. Noble metals based nano-electrocatalysts illustrate the unique hydrogen evolution reaction efficiency that due to their scarcity and high cost are not selective candidates [7,8]. Recently, there are many researches on other earth-abundant and low-cost electrocatalysts with promising activity and stability [9,10], particularly in transition metals-based oxides [11,12], phosphides [13,14], carbides [15], nitrides [16], perovskites [17] and sulfides [18,19]. In recent years, the binary and ternary transition metal sulfides including NiS [20], Ni3S2 [21], CoS [22], Co9S8 [23] and NiCo2S4 [24] have been considered as suitable electrode materials for electrochemical processes due to their thermal and mechanical stability, high capacitance and electrical conductivity. Nickel cobalt sulfide (NiCo2S4) has been paid more and more attention as a satisfactory electrode material. NiCo2S4 nanostructures have higher electric conductivity about 100 times and lower band gap in comparison with corresponding NiCo2O4 nanostructures [25]. Moreover, ternary NiCo2S4 nanostructures can offer more redox reaction with two Co3+ and Ni2+ ions than binary sulfide nanostructures, following that better electrochemical efficiency [26]. Nickel cobalt sulfide structures exist with diverse shapes including nanoparticle [27], nanotube [28], hallow sphere [29] and nanoflake [30], which were synthesized via different chemical routes. Additionally, due to poor rate proficiency and electrochemical stability of ternary transition metal sulfides, engineering nanomaterial as an operative way to develop the electrochemical performance is an crucial strategy to make sure the great electroactive sites and fast electron/ion diffusion route. In order to solve this problem, carbon-based nanocomposites as conductive substrates are applied such as carbon nanotube networks [31], graphene sheets [32], and carbon nitrides [33]. In particular, a two-dimensional (2D) sheet, such as graphene quantum dot (GQDs) with sp2-bonded carbon atoms and a high surface area, can be coupled with ternary transition metal sulfides-based materials via its functional groups, endowing the hybrid nanostructures with distinctive structural characteristics and synergistic electrochemical properties derived from both counterparts. Newly, functionalized GQDs with significant physicochemical properties have emerged for diverse operational programs, especially in the field of energy storage. There are few reports on the use of GQDs in the energy storage platform [34,35].However, the application of these carbon-based compounds in the electrochemical energy storage devices exposed including supercapacitor, Li-ion batteries, Na-ion and Li-S batteries [36–38]. On the other hand, carbon microspheres have favorable features in energy applications [39]. In current work, 3D hierarchical sand-rose shaped N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shell spheres were fabricated to achieving enhanced electrochemical hydrogen storage capacity. Ni-Co sulfide based materials were synthesized through one step hydrothermal approach and TGA as the both sulfur source and capping agent. Due to notable effect of surface properties of obtained nanostructures on energy storage applications, diverse experimental circumstances were examined. The created nanostructures were evaluated in terms of crystallinity structure, composition, surface porosity and morphology. Finally, different NCS nanostructures were analyzed to hydrogen storage capacity that 3D hierarchical sand-rose shaped N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shell spheres has the best performance due to synergistic effects of derived from three components of N-GQDs, CS and Ni-Co sulfides.

Porous carbon microspheres were created through quick sonochemical approach in the existence of formaldehyde solution (37% Wt. in H2O stabilized by 10% methanol) and resorcinol powder (99% purity, Sigma-Aldrich). For preparation, 1 g resorcinol was dissolved in 1.4 ml formaldehyde under stirring and then obtained solution was diluted in H2O-Ethanol solvent mixture. After obtaining of homogeneous mixture, 0.5 ml NH3 solution was added to solution under ultra-sonication irradiation to achieve milky white solution. All reaction steps are carried out in the ice bath. The reaction temperature controlled at 20 °C. The prepared R-F polymer resin was collected using centrifugation and dried at 70 °C. The synthesized brown resin was calcined at 900 °C for 2 h with N2 atmosphere [40]. Diverse parameters of synthesis of carbon spheres have been studied in previous literature including R/F ratio, solvent and catalyst concentration [41]. In this work for the first time, the effect of basic catalysts such as ethylene diamine (en), triethylene tetraamine (TETA) and tetra ethylene pentaamine (TEPA) was considered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of N-Doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs with suitable function groups were prepared through hydrothermal carbonization of citric acid as carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen dopant. In order to simple synthesis, proper amount of citric acid and ethylene diamine are utilized in hydrothermal method applying temperature of 180 °C for 24 h. More details for preparation of N-GQDs have been considered in previous literature [42].

In the current work, NCS nanostructures were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method in the presence of tioglycolic acid (TGA), for the first time. All chemical reagents for synthesis of NCS nanostructures have the highest purity available from Merck Company. Ni and Co salts (Ni:Co molar ratio 1:2) were dissolved in distilled water under stirring. Following that, TGA as a sulfur precursor was added to previous cationic solution. For adjusting of pH, NaOH solution was poured slowly and color of mixture changed from pink to brown. The obtained solution was heated 180 °C during 24 h in a stainless steel autoclave. The performed reaction details in different circumstances have been presented in Table 1 .

For preparation of carbon based core-shell via in-situ approach, optimized NCS nanostructures are created similar method presented in “section 2.3.”. Briefly, Ni(OAC)2·6H2O, Co(OAC)2·6H2O and TGA are dissolved in distilled water in adjusted pH. On the other hand, the created carbon microspheres with en-catalyst are ultrasonically dispersed in water. Both previous solutions are combined together and moved to stainless steel autoclave and preserved at 180 °C for 24 h. the as-synthesized powder is dried in oven after centrifugation and labeled NCS/CS core-shell. In order to prepare sand-rose shaped N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shell, N-GQDs are dispersed in the ultrasonic bath and added to prepare NCS/CS powder, simultaneously. After 30 min, the final product is collected and dried which denote as N-GQDs/NCS/CS. The formation process of 3D hierarchical sand-rose shaped N-GQDs/NCS/CS spheres illustrates in the Fig. 14.

The crystallinity, phase and chemical composition of the nano-products were documented using a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR; Nicolet Magna-550 spectrophotometer in KBr pellets ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1), and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Philips-Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation; λ = 0.15418 nm; scanning range 10 < 2θ < 80). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) descriptions were obtained on a Philips EM208 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV for structural observation in the nanoscale. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was achieved in an LEO-1455VP equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. The physical properties of the samples were performed using a Brunauer–Emmett–Teller–Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BET-BJH) (which applied at −196 °C, Tristar 3000, Micromeritics), and a vibrating sample magnetometer 60 (VSM, Meghnatis Kavir Kashan Co., Kashan, Iran). Finally, the electrochemical measurements were performed by the potentiostat/galvanostat (SAMA 500, electroanalyzer system, I.R. Iran) in three electrodes system include working electrode, platinum plate as a counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode and KOH (2 M) electrolyte at 1 mAg−1 current density. The capacity for all systems was formulated using the following equation:
(1) Capacity = [Current (mA) × Time (h)]/Active Mass (g)
The cyclic voltammograms and EIS tests for as-fabricated electrodes were conducted in the similar condition.

Porous carbon spheres derived from resorcinol-formaldehyde (R-F) gels have various applications because of their favorable porosity and electro-conductivity. Therefore, numerous methods have been developed to form of carbon microspheres. These routs need the long time and supercritical conditions, but the used method in this work is fast in room temperature circumstances. Rapid condensation of resorcinol and formaldehyde occur under ultrasonic irradiations in the presence of different basic catalysts. Different basic catalysts such as en, TETA and TEPA compared to NH3 were utilized, in order to control of spheres size. Also, ultrasound irradiation with production of active species (OH• and H•) help to the formation of hydroxymethyl-substituted samples and following that, fast condensation n and polymerization of R-F resin and finally, with thermal treatment were created carbon microsphere.

Fig. 1 i(a–d) illustrate FT-IR spectra of R-F resins in the existence of different amine catalysts for initiation of the polymerization process. As shown in all spectra, the wide peak about 3180 cm−1 is assigned to O–H groups of absorbed water. The available peaks at 2920, 2850 and 1449 cm−1 are related to stretching and scissor pulsations. The sharp peak at 1413 cm−1 is signified to the aromatic groups (carbonyl functional groups). The existing peaks in 1000–1300 cm−1 region are corresponded to C–O–C groups [43]. After calcination process under 900 °C and nitrogen atmosphere, all functional groups are removed and carbon sphere are created [44]. As demonstrated in Fig. 1ii(a–d), all the absorption peaks are disappeared, but in the presence of TETA and TEPA, a small amount of water and organic groups remain on the sample surface. Fig. 1iii(a–d) represent XRD patterns of amorphous CSs prepared in the presence of several amine catalysts. In all patterns, two broad peaks at 2theta of 25 and 43° are related to (002) and (101) plans, respectively.

In order to morphology consideration, size and regularity of as-synthesized carbon spheres were compared in acquired FE-SEM images (Fig. 2 (a–d)). The prepared microspheres in the ethylene diamine-catalyzed reaction have the most uniformity and smallest size (280–600 nm), but microspheres with NH3, TETA and TEPA are agglomerated. It is proposed that less release rate of OH− in ethylene diamine effectively decreases the growth rate and controls the sphere size. Besides, the small contribution of the particles are merged because of en-catalyzed sonication procedure with a favorable release rate of OH− that seen clearly in Fig. 2b. This fusion cause better correlation of spheres and more electron conduction and finally high hydrogen storage capacity. Fig. 2e represents TEM picture of optimized en-catalyzed carbon spheres with approximate size of 200 nm which sphere integration was observed, clearly. The inset picture display HRTEM of carbonized R-F resin in 900 °C that its coarse and opaque surface characterizes amorphous carbon spheres. Fig. 2f display EDS analysis of CSs that peak associated with C is undoubtedly detected and provide robust confirmation for CSs sample without any impurity.

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isothermal measurement was exerted to identify the porosity properties of carbon microspheres that has been displayed in Fig. 3 a. The isotherms exhibit the type IV isotherm with a H4 hysteresis loop at the relative pressure P/P0 ranging from 0.0 to 1.0. This curve is a characteristic of the different processes between adsorption into and desorption from the agglomerated solids with uniform size and shape [45]. The porosity characteristics of the samples such as surface areas, pore volumes, and pore sizes have been reported in Table 2 . The inset figure (Fig. 3a) corresponding pore size distribution of carbon microspheres. The pore size distributions are between 1 and 10 nm. It is perceived that the surface area of carbon spheres provided with en catalyst is higher than other catalysts. According to the achieved data from Table 2 and Fig. 3a, the synthesized carbon with different alkaline catalysts are microporous materials with average size < 2 nm. Also, due to high specific surface area are favorable candidates for electrochemical applications.

The electrochemical behaviors of CSs catalyzed with four linear amine types compared through cyclic voltammetry technique. Fig. 3b exhibits the CV curves for the CS1, CS2, CS3 and CS4 over a potential region of −1.0 to −0.2 V. The electrode responses (anodic and cathodic peaks) for all samples have been listed in Table 3 . The CS2 sample express better electrode response than others. Therefore, the en-catalyzed carbon spheres were selected as optimum support for applying in next step of electrode designing. Monotonous pore diameters (1.73 nm) can accelerate diffusion route of ions; secondly, its specific surface region as large as 891.62 m2/g was principally contributed by the mesopores. Functional economical catalysts are necessitated, which should have excellent electrochemical activity as well. Since porous structures and materials of carbon spheres are inherently useful for electrochemical applications, manipulation of the complementary components with special porous structures is required to attain boosted electrochemical performances [39].

The obtained N-GQDs have three nitrogen types of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphite N. The pyrrolic N are formed from dehydrolysis between the neighbor carboxyl and amide groups in the five membered ring. Graphite N originates from the Nitrogen atoms that are bonded with 3 neighbor Carbon atoms. Pyridinic-N is substituted into the hexagonal ring via one σ and π bonds with the neighbor carbon [46]. The archived brown solution denote as N-GQDs which used for composition and bond structure characterization. FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs in Fig. 4 a confirms development of COOH and C–N functional groups on the GQDs surface. The peaks at 1655, 1560, 1386 cm−1 are related to CO, CC and C–N bonds. The existing peaks at 700–1000 cm−1 are assigned to C–H vibrations [42]. Fig. 4b elucidates XRD pattern of N-GQDs with the broad peak at 2theta of 25° which affirms the amorphous carbon texture. The EDS datum of product in Fig. 4c with peaks of C and N certify construction of pure N-GQDs.

X-ray diffractograms were achieved for verification of the purity and crystalline structure of NCS nanostructures. According to previous researches, the blank test (NCS0) was conducted with sulfur source of Na2S that its XRD diffractogram has been presented in Fig. 5 a. In the mentioned diffractogram has not suitable crystallinity. Therefore, TGA as a novel sulfur source was applied with divers molar ratios of Ni:TGA (1:20, 1:30 and 1:40) and their XRD diffractograms have been shown in Fig. 5b–d, respectively. In all diffractograms, the available peaks in 2θ = 16.34, 26.83, 31.59, 38.32, 47.41, 50.46 and 55.33° are assigned to (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) plans of cubic nickel cobalt sulfide (JCPDS = 20-0782) with Fd3m space group. According to Fig. 5b, the NCS1 texture comprises NiCo2S4, Co9S8 and CoS crystals which the small peaks in 2θ = 31, 35.5, 47.3 and 54.5° are related to hexagonal CoS component (JCPDS = 75-0605) and distinct peaks at 2θ = 29.8 and 52° are assigned to cubic Co9S8 (JCPDS = 75-2023). Both NiCo2S4 and CoS compounds are observed in NCS2 and NCS3 specimens (Fig. 5c, d).

In Fig. 5e, NCS4 sample prepared through Ni(OAC)2·6H2O and Co(OAC)2·6H2O has three sulfide based of NiCo2S4, CoS and Co9S8. Fig. 5f confirms pure cubic NiCo2S4 (NCS5) provided utilizing Ni(Cl)2·6H2O and Co(Cl)2·6H2O without any impurity corresponding to other forms of cobalt and nickel sulfide. The average crystallite size of NCS specimens calculated by the Scherer equation [47] has been demonstrated in Table 1. Fig. 5g, h illustrate XRD diffractograms of NCS samples fabricated by changing pH. NCS6 structures synthesized with pH = 7 (3 g NaOH) is pure NiCo2S4 nanocrystal (Fig. 5g). As demonstrated in Fig. 5h (NCS7), in prepared sample with pH = 14 (5 g NaOH), cubic Co9S8 (JCPDS = 75-2023) exist in large quantities along with NiCo2S4. The complete information about formation components of different experimental conditions have summarized in Table 1.

FE-SEM micrographs of NCS1, NCS2 and NCS3 nanostructures have been exposed in Fig. 5i–k. In accordance with Fig. 5i1, 2, NCS1 microspheres composed of nanoparticles (15–70 nm) have dimensions of 0.5–2 μm with Ni:TGA molar ratio of 1:20 (4 ml TGA). Fig. 5j1, 2 display morphology and size of NCS2 microspheres formed from petal-like components in the presence of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and Co(NO3)2·6H2O and Ni:TGA molar ratio of 1:30 (5 ml TGA). The diameter of microsphere is approximately 2 μm and thickness of nano-petals is about 10 nm. 3D hierarchical microspheres of NCS3 were synthesized using 6 ml TGA, regularly. FE-SEM micrographs of related sample in Fig. 5k1, 2 demonstrate microspheres (diameter = 1–2 μm) composed of nanoplate with width of ∼20 nm. According to the previous descriptions, in low concentration of TGA, irregular nanoparticle were formed. NCS2 is optimum model for synthesis of uniform nano-petal structures in terms of TGA amount. Raising the amount of TGA will increase the percentage of free TGA precursor which could simply form hydrogen bond with themselves and finally formation of nano-plate with larger thickness. TGA with having active functional groups as a capping agent help to growth of space-limited nano-plates.

The effect of precursor salts form on the fabricated NCS shapes has been elucidated in Fig. 5l, m. As presented in Fig. 5l1, 2, NCS4 prepared using Ni(OAC)2·6H2O and Co(OAC)2·6H2O have composed of hexagonal nanosheets with diameter of 0.5 μm and thickness of 20 nm. Fig. 5m1, 2 demonstrate NiCo2S4 microflowers synthesized with chloride sources that nanosheets (thickness ∼25 nm) have grown together, uniformly. Overall, acetate salts have higher power to dissociation of hydrogen of TGA and releasing S2−, nucleation and growth are carried out faster and finally more irregular morphology is formed.

Non-porous agglomerated structures were formed for NCS6 and NCS7 samples formed in pH = 10 and 14 which illustrated in Fig. 5n, o, respectively. Porous open structures composed of nano-plate with limited thickness are observed in the high magnification FE-SEM of all NCS nanostructures. These porous structures are valuable in order to operative electron transport in electrolyte-electrode boundary for hydrogen storage applications [48].

Optimized sand-rose shaped NCS spheres hydrothermally designed were corroborated with techniques of FT-IR and EDS that have been explained in Fig. 6 a, b. The peaks centered around 400–1000 cm−1 may be assigned to Ni-S and Co-S symmetric and asymmetric stretching shakings [49]. The peaks at 3450 and 1630 cm−1 may be matched to surface-OH groups. The EDS pattern provides reconfirmation for production of NCS4 spheres (see Fig. 6b). The representative peaks of Ni, Co and S are perceived.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves of selected samples (NCS2, NCS4, NCS5 and NCS7) have been established in Fig. 6c. The obtained results from BET analysis have been abbreviated in Table 4 . As illustrated in Fig. 6c, the N2 isotherms of NCS structures belong to a type-IV and H4 hysteresis that related to irregular shape particles with broad size distribution [45]. According to the Table 4 information, ternary NiCo2S4/CoS/Co9S8 flower-like spheres (NCS4) have the largest specific surface area and the smallest pore diameter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NCS/CS core-shell and N-GQDs/NCS/ CS core-double shell
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 a elucidates XRD pattern of sand-rose shaped N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shell that confirm presence of NiCo2S4 ((JCPDS = 20-0782), CoS (JCPDS = 75-0605) and Co9S8 (JCPDS = 75-2023) along with carbon components of carbon sphere and graphene quantum dots. Two broad peak around 25 and 45° are attributed to carbon samples. Fig. 7b, c display EDS spectroscopy of NCS/CS and N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-shell for verification of elemental compositions. In addition to presence of Ni, Co and S for confirmation of NCS nanostructures, C and N indicate existing of CS and N-GQDs in NCS/CS and N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-shell, respectively. Fig. 8 a, b shows the FE-SEM of NCS/CS core-shell with sand-rose shape. The covered NCS/CS structures by N-GQDs demonstrates in the FE-SEM micrographs of Fig. 8 c, d. Actually, the N-GQDs as second shell can adsorbed on the surface of the first shell of NCS through surface functional group and changing in the surface morphology is quite obvious. Detailed microstructure of the final core-double shell is monitored in TEM and HR-TEM microscopic images (Fig. 8e-h). The structure of designed core-double shell has been determined in the Fig. 8e which confirms the presence of two shells on the CS core. As shown in Fig. 8g, h, the lattice fringes can be seen clearly with the space of 0.175, 0.203 and 0.299 nm in line with (440), (422) and (311) crystal plans of Co9S8. The lattice fringes of 0.283, 0.163, 0.234 nm can readily be indexed to the (311), (440) and (400) crystal plans of NiCo2S4. The lattice fringe of 0.149 nm is reasonably ascribed to the interplanar distance of the (103) crystalline plane of CoS. The mentioned considerations acknowledge that the three phases of Co9S8, NiCo2S4 and CoS are well-dispersed with homogeneous hetero-interfaces at the nanoscale.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves of NCS/CS and N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-shell have been revealed in Fig. 9 a, b. The attained outcomes from BET investigation have been summarized in Table 4. The hysteresis type of both nanocomposites are related to aggregate spheres with slit-like pores with broad size distribution (type-IV and H4) [45]. According to the Table 4 data, ternary sand-rose shaped N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shell has better porosity properties than binary NCS/CS core-shell and finally, is selected as a favorable candidate for electrochemical hydrogen storage.

The room temperature hysteresis loops of NiCo2S4 nanostructures and NCS/CS and N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-shell were considered via a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) in an applied magnetic field from −15,000 to 15,000 Oe that presented in Fig. 9c. All samples indicate ferromagnetic behavior. Magnetic parameters of as-synthesized samples have been summarized in Table 5 . As observed in Table 5, all magnetic factors of NiCo2S4 nanoparticles are higher than carbon based core shell. While with the introduction of N-GQDs to NCS/CS structures, magnetic moments increase. Because N-GQDs with the high edge-to-area ratio have significant spin polarized edge situations. Also, the zigzag edge atoms or vacancies are all thought to cause magnetic properties [50].

As shown in Fig. 9d, the TG curve for three selected samples ascertains three weight loss platforms. In the sample N-GQDs/NCS4/CS, the first obvious mass losses of 2.93% below 300 °C may be due to the volatilization of absorbed water and surface adsorbed precursors. The second weight losses of 6.73% at about 300–700 °C correspond to the removal of oxygen-containing functional groups such as COOH, OH and CO of the N-GQDs. Obviously, there is a sharp weight loss (3.05%) at about 700–800 °C, which may be ascribed to the thermal decomposition of NiCo2S4 due to the partial loss of sulfur. Also, the weight loss step in this range may be related to the conversion of sulfides to oxides. Suggesting that the phase of NiCo2S4 had changed, which indicates the oxidation of metal sulfides to metal sulfate and metal oxysulfate (MSO4 and MOSO4).

The electrochemical characteristics of NCS2, NCS4, NCS5, NCS7, NCS/CS and N-GQDs/NCS/CSs samples were verified through a set of CVs in 2.0 M KOH solution (at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1) and three-electrodes of working, counter (Pt) and reference (Ag/AgCl). As shown in Fig. 10 , the cyclic voltammograms of all samples as the active materials for hydrogen sorption demonstrate reversible curve within the potential range −1.0 to −0.2 V. The summarized anodic and cathodic peak response has been reported in Table 6 . These parameters for pristine NCS5 is less than other samples which including CoS or Co9S8 in their texture. It is interesting to note that presence of Co9S8 and CoS in combination with NCS structures, can alter the electrochemical attributes of active materials. As a consequence, the two potential peaks in both anodic and cathodic processed for all samples appreciably reflected desirable electrochemical characteristic for hydrogen storage. Moreover, cathodic peak is related to adsorption of hydrogen on active material and anodic peak can be attributed to reversible electrochemical hydrogen adsorption/desorption reaction occurring on the fabricated electrodes. The CV test repeated 10 cycles for all samples. Peaks area expansion with cycling is owing to the enhancing volume of electrode materials. The electrochemical oxidation for pristine NCS was stopped after 10 CV cycles since almost stable curves were acquired. However, the current for other samples increase during 10 cycles which show the formation of more active sites. Increasing the active site in the electrode materials as suitable electrocatalyst led to ideal electrochemical performance [51]. The electrode response for NCS/CS and N-GQDs/NCS/CS core shell electrodes show better results than those of NCS based compounds. Presence of CS and N-GQDs in the texture of core-double shell microspheres led to high anodic and cathodic current which reflected the favorable conductivity and ability for redox process in the designed active materials.

The materials structure and composition have a great effect on the electrochemical behaviors and capacity of fabricated electrodes in the electrochemical cells which assembled for energy storage application. Fig. 11 a-d compare the discharge profiles for sulfide based electrodes synthesized in different conditions which led to various composition and structures. The discharge capacity for pristine NiCo2S4 (NCS5) with morphology of regular sheets after 20 cycles was measured 751 mAh g−1. The discharge capacity of 20th cycle for NiCo2S4/CoS (NCS2) with petal-like morphology recorded 654 mAh g−1. The fabricated Co9S8/NCS sample in pH = 14 (NCS7) with morphology of agglomerated plates displays the initial cycle to 20th cycle which measured capacity from 187 to 856 mAh g−1. The discharge capacity of NCS/Co9S8/CoS sample prepared using acetate precursor (NCS4) at 20th cycles evaluated 898 mAh g−1 with morphology of hexagonal nanosheets. In this present comparison, a study of the electrochemical hydrogen storage ability has been developed for pristine NiCo2S4, NiCo2S4/CoS, Co9S8/NiCo2S4 and NiCo2S4/Co9S8/CoS specimens. The achieved results in the Fig. 11 confirm that the NiCo2S4/Co9S8/CoS compounds are more active compared to the pristine NiCo2S4 structures for hydrogen adsorption. The existence of Co9S8 or CoS along with NiCO2S4 can alter the electrochemical behaviors for energy storage due to cooperation of electrocatalytic effect and conductivity in the texture of active materials. Basu reported the hydrogen evolution catalyst behaviors of Co9S8, NiCo2S4 and NiCo2S4/Co9S8 hetero-structures. The results of this research confirmed the positive effect of Co9S8 on increasing the conductivity and catalytic impact of NiCo2S4 materials [52].

Metal sulfides of the chalcogenides family are usual components for electrochemical hydrogen storage. The morphology of metal sulphides plays a critical role in the capability of hydrogen storage [53]. By changing the Oxygen of NiCo2O4 to Sulphur, the NiCo2S4 compounds could be formed with many attributes such as the flexible framework and structure for easy electrons transportation, high electric conductivity than that of NiCo2O4. Such attributes cause NiCo2S4 possess supreme specific active materials for energy storage application [54].

The redox reaction occur during charge/discharge process in the electrode comprise of sand-rose shaped NiCo2S4/Co9S8/CoS structures which summarized as follows [24,55];
(2)
NiCo2S4⇛ NiS + OH− ⇄ NiSOH + e−
(3) CoS + OH− ⇄ CoSOH + e−
(4) CoSOH + OH− ⇄ CoSO + H2O + e−
(5)
Co9S8 ⇛ Co9S8 + OH− ⇄ Co9S8OH + e−
(6) Co9S8OH + OH− ⇄ Co9S8O + H2O + e−
(7)
CoS ⇛ CoS + OH− ⇄ CoSOH + e−
(8) CoSOH + OH− ⇄ CoSO + H2O + e−

On the other hands, the structures of fabricated samples have significant effect on the electrochemical hydrogen storage ability. The structural properties can influence the electrolyte/electrode contact area, the electrolyte flowing and ion transportation, the reaction active sites, and finally affect the performance. The prepared active materials in this work have 3D hierarchical sand-rose structures which comprise 2D plates. The hierarchical structures show considerable and promising electrochemical efficiency due to the great active specific surface area and short electron and ion transport directions. Their supreme surface areas not only increment the electro-active locations, but also mitigate volume conversion in the charge–discharge proceeding. With the avails of high electronic conductivity, the interconnected nano-sized subunits of the compound and the presence of pores, which reduced the space for electrolyte ions transport, the 3D hierarchical materials present remarkable specific capacitance. The 2D nano-sheets delivered high mass loading and significant electrochemical ability. Also, in this work, the 2D nano-plates were assembled on the surface of carbon spheres in order to creation of 3D hierarchical sand-rose spheres [56–58]. The 3D hierarchical morphologies display considerable specifications owing to both mechanical and electrochemical proficiency. Design of 3D structures owing to structural durability, excellent surface area and self-aggregation prohibition enable a wonderful situation for potential energy storgae materials [59,60]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of carbon sphere as physical support
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon spheres generally express high surface area and acceptable electrical conductivity, which seem to be desire substrate materials for physical support of NCS structures. Carbon sphere as a core and support for 3D hierarchical NCS microsphere and N-GQDs shell materials play remarkable role in boosting the electrical conductivity and electronic transportation procedure as well as ensuring the synergetic effects between carbon sphere and NCS shell. CSs equip electrically conducting ways within the electrode for the rapid electron transfer and electrolyte-ion penetration in the time of charge-discharge proceeding [61,62]. After decorating with NCS structures, the sand-rose shaped NCS/CS core-shell electrodes present the high capacitance (930 mAh g−1) that has been presented in Fig. 12 a.

Discharge profiles for 3D hierarchical sand-rose shaped N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shells have been illustrated in Fig. 12b for 20 cycles. The discharge capacity for designed N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shell electrode material was calculated 1244 mAh g−1 after 20 cycles. The results certified the positive effect of N-GQDs on the NCS/CS core-shell which leads to increasing the hydrogen storage proficiency. The hydrogen storage capability for 3D hierarchical sand-rose shaped N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shell is better than pristine NCS4 (898 mAh g−1) and NCS/CS (930 mAh g−1) core-shell owing to rapid electron transfer in the N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shell electrodes. The results confirmed the positive effect of N-GQDs on the pristine NCS and NCS/CS microstructures which leads to enhancing the hydrogen storage performance. The energy storage ability for N-GQDs based core-shell is better than pristine NCS and NCS/CS microstructures due to the electron transfer in the N-GQDs based electrodes is fairly rapid. The forming N-GQDs on NCS/CS electrodes can modify the discharge capacity, because of the abundant edge areas prepared by tiny sized N-GQDs and the suitable conductivity correlated to NCS bond structures. The high performance of N-GQDs based core-shell can be elucidating due to the trap states formed by both dopants and edge states which can adsorb charge carriers to increase the storage capability. The electron density of graphene layers can be modified trough the doping by electron acceptor or electron donor species. The Nitrogen as heteroatoms in the graphene structure can replace with carbon atoms. Therefore, electron migration occurs which leads to creating the electron and hole. This structure altering with cooperating N-GQDs play a great role in electrochemical and electrocatalyst systems for energy storage applications [63,64]. On the other hand, nitrogen and carbon have similar radii and compact electronegativity. Also, the Nitrogen as a donor atom with ability in the electronic modification of graphene can improve the conductivity and catalysis behaviors of graphene structure [65,66].

In the N-GQDs structure, the Nitrogen plays two important roles. (i) The pyridinic nitrogen-oxide form the electrophilic sites through the formation of electron-rich carbon atoms (adjacent carbon) which simplify the hydrogen adsorption. Then, the adsorbed hydrogen comes out to form of H2 from the active site by the attempt of another H+ and the active site is rebuilt. (ii) The Nitrogen can fabricate the interconnected construction to increase the conductivity [67–75]. The N-interlinkages among the graphene quantum dots are owing to its modified stability and electro-catalytic issue. The interconnected construction of graphene quantum dots is especially active towards hydrogen evolution reaction without any other conducting material support. Briefly, connection of small N-GQDs by suitable linkers led to functional and effective materials for hydrogen evolution and hydrogen storage application. The N-GQDs system with electron rich and the interconnected structure brings in developed conductivity and the pyridinic-N-oxide creates electrophilic sites, thus helping the H+ absorption on the material. Thus synthesized GQDs unveil novel outlook in the electro-catalysis field based on graphene compounds.

In order to studying the conductivity of final active material which prepared in the texture of core-double shell microspheres, the EIS technique was performed within the frequency range of 0.1 Hz to 100 KHz at open circuit potential. Also, the conductivity of copper substrate as a bare electrode was inquire in the same way. The obtained Nyquist curve (Fig. 13
) for both bare and N-GQDs/NCS/CSs electrodes include a semicircle and a subsequent inclined line at high and low frequency respectively. The fitted value of transfer resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) for N-GQDs/NCS/CS electrode measured about 249 and 2429 Ohm, respectively. As can be seen, the diameter for semicircle of N-GQDs/NCS/CS is lower than copper substrate which confirmed the low transfer resistance (Rct) and high conductivity for electrode prepared by N-GQDs/NCS/CS. The equivalent circuit compatible with the Nyquist diagram is depicted in the inset of Fig. 13, where Rs is the ohmic resistance of the solution and electrode, Rct is the charge transfer resistance, W is a Warburg impedance, and C is the capacitor element.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (i)
ce:list-item.ce:para: In summary, the 3D hierarchical sand-rose shaped spheres in the form of core-double shell designed through decoration of Ni-Co sulfide as the first shell and N-GQDs as the second shell on the CSs substrate. Ni or Co salt, pH and TGA ratio selected as operational parameters which affect on the morphology, size and composition of sulfide base shell. The best electrochemical performance in the terms of hydrogen storage acquired using NCS/CoS/Co9S8 as active materials with discharge capacity of 898 mAh g−1 compared to NCS, NCS/CoS or Co9S8/NCS compounds.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (ii)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Carbon microspheres as physical support and conductive agent ultrasonically synthesized which utilized in the core of designed electrode materials. Decoration of CSs by sand-rose shaped NCS/CoS/Co9S8 nanostructures can reveal the hydrogen storage capacity up to 930 mAh g−1.

ce:list-item.ce:label: (iii)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots as the second shell covered the fabricated structure of NCS/CS which developed the electrochemical behaviors of the final designed electrode in the hydrogen sorption (1244 mAh g−1). By comparison of EIS results of bare and modified electrode by 3D hierarchical N-GQDs/NCS/CS spheres, a significant increasing in the designed electrode is verifiable. These altering electrochemical outcomes obtained due to the potential effect of N-GQDs in terms of improvement of conductivity and creation of trap sites for hydrogen sorption.
